title: Box Model
question: Explain the CSS box model.
answer: Every element is a rectangular box composed of content, padding, border, and margin. `box-sizing: border-box` includes padding+border in the declared width/height.
explanation: The default `content-box` is a common source of layout surprises.
codeExample: * { box-sizing: border-box; }
interviewTips: Draw the box diagram.

title: Flexbox vs Grid
question: When should you use Flexbox vs CSS Grid?
answer: Flexbox is 1-dimensional (row OR column). Grid is 2-dimensional (rows AND columns). Use Grid for page layout, Flex for component-level alignment.
explanation: They compose beautifully — a Grid parent with Flex children is common.
codeExample:
.grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr); gap: 1rem; }
.flex { display: flex; align-items: center; gap: .5rem; }
bestPractices: Use gap over margins for spacing.
interviewTips: Explain minmax, auto-fit/auto-fill in Grid.

title: Specificity
question: How is CSS specificity calculated?
answer: Specificity is a tuple (inline, ids, classes/attrs/pseudo-classes, elements). Higher wins; ties resolved by order.
explanation: !important overrides normal rules but should be a last resort.
bestPractices: Keep selectors flat; avoid deep nesting.
interviewTips: Explain cascade layers (@layer) as a modern tool.

title: Position: relative vs absolute vs fixed vs sticky
question: Compare the CSS position values.
answer: static (default), relative (offset from itself), absolute (from nearest positioned ancestor), fixed (from viewport), sticky (relative until crossing a threshold).
explanation: sticky is great for section headers that pin as you scroll.
codeExample: .header { position: sticky; top: 0; }
bestPractices: Set a positioned ancestor before using absolute inside.
commonMistakes: Sticky breaking due to overflow:hidden ancestors.
interviewTips: Show a sticky nav example.

title: Responsive Design
question: What are the pillars of responsive design?
answer: Fluid layouts, flexible images, media queries, and mobile-first thinking.
explanation: Combine relative units (%, rem, vw), min/max-width media queries, and modern tools like container queries.
codeExample: @container (min-width: 500px) { .card { grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; } }
interviewTips: Discuss container queries as a modern tool.

title: CSS Custom Properties (Variables)
question: How do CSS custom properties work and how do they differ from Sass variables?
answer: Custom properties (`--name`) are live values read with `var()`. Unlike Sass variables (compile-time), they are dynamic at runtime, inherit through the cascade, and can be changed with JavaScript or media queries.
explanation: Because they cascade and inherit, defining them on `:root` makes them global, while redefining on a component scopes them locally. They enable theming without preprocessors.
interviewTips: Mention runtime theming and JS access via setProperty.

title: Pseudo-classes vs Pseudo-elements
question: What is the difference between a pseudo-class and a pseudo-element?
answer: A pseudo-class (`:hover`, `:nth-child`) targets an element in a particular state. A pseudo-element (`::before`, `::first-line`) styles a specific part of an element or injects generated content.
explanation: Modern CSS uses double colons for pseudo-elements to distinguish them, though single colon still works for legacy ones. Pseudo-elements require `content` to render ::before/::after.
interviewTips: Give one example of each in the same answer.

title: z-index and Stacking Context
question: Why does z-index sometimes not work as expected?
answer: z-index only applies to positioned elements and only within the same stacking context. A new stacking context (from transform, opacity < 1, filter, will-change, etc.) traps children, so a high z-index inside cannot escape a lower-ranked parent context.
explanation: Understanding what creates a stacking context is key: it is not just position + z-index. Opacity, transforms, and flex/grid items with z-index all create one.
interviewTips: List properties that create a stacking context.

title: Transitions vs Animations
question: When should you use a CSS transition versus a keyframe animation?
answer: Transitions animate between two states triggered by a change (hover, class toggle). Animations use `@keyframes` for multi-step, looping, or self-starting motion that does not need a trigger.
explanation: Transitions are simpler for state changes; animations give fine control over intermediate frames, iteration count, and direction.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to define a trigger for transitions.
interviewTips: Explain which properties are cheap to animate.

title: CSS Transforms
question: What are CSS transforms and why are they performant?
answer: `transform` applies translate, rotate, scale, and skew. They are GPU-accelerated and do not trigger layout/reflow, only compositing, making them ideal for smooth animation.
explanation: Because transforms operate on a composited layer, they avoid expensive reflow that properties like top/left cause. transform-origin controls the pivot point.
bestPractices: Prefer transform over top/left for motion.
commonMistakes: Forgetting transforms create a stacking context.
interviewTips: Tie transforms to the rendering pipeline (layout/paint/composite).

title: em vs rem vs px
question: What is the difference between em, rem, and px units?
answer: `px` is an absolute pixel. `em` is relative to the element's own font-size (compounds when nested). `rem` is relative to the root (`html`) font-size, avoiding compounding.
explanation: rem gives predictable, accessible scaling tied to user root settings, while em is useful for component-local spacing that should scale with local font-size.
bestPractices: Use rem for typography and layout scale.
Avoid fixed px for font sizes to respect user zoom.
commonMistakes: Nesting em and getting compounding surprises.
interviewTips: Explain compounding with a nested em example.

title: clamp(), min(), and max()
question: How do the CSS math functions clamp(), min(), and max() work?
answer: `min()` picks the smallest of its arguments, `max()` the largest, and `clamp(min, preferred, max)` locks a value between bounds. They enable fluid, responsive sizing without media queries.
explanation: clamp() is ideal for fluid typography: the middle value scales with the viewport while min and max cap it. They accept mixed units.
bestPractices: Use clamp for fluid type instead of many media queries.
Combine a vw term with a rem term for readable scaling.
commonMistakes: Swapping the min and max arguments.
Using only vw, breaking readability at extremes.
interviewTips: Show a fluid font-size clamp expression.

title: The Cascade and Inheritance
question: How do the cascade and inheritance determine applied styles?
answer: The cascade resolves competing declarations by origin/importance, then specificity, then source order. Inheritance passes certain properties (color, font) from parent to child; non-inherited ones use their initial value unless set.
explanation: Use `inherit`, `initial`, `unset`, and `revert` to control this explicitly. Layers (@layer) now sit above specificity in the cascade order.
interviewTips: Order the cascade steps precisely.

title: Container Queries
question: What are container queries and how do they differ from media queries?
answer: Container queries style an element based on the size of its containing element rather than the viewport. You set `container-type` on a parent, then query it with `@container`.
explanation: This makes components truly reusable: a card adapts to its slot whether in a sidebar or full width, independent of the overall screen size.
bestPractices: Use container queries for reusable components.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to set container-type on the parent.
Querying the element itself instead of an ancestor container.
interviewTips: Contrast component-relative vs viewport-relative sizing.

title: Sticky Positioning Gotchas
question: Why does position: sticky sometimes fail to stick?
answer: Sticky requires a threshold (top/bottom/left/right), a scrollable ancestor, and no ancestor with overflow: hidden/auto/scroll that clips it. It also only sticks within its parent's bounds.
explanation: A common failure is an ancestor with `overflow: hidden`, which silently disables sticky. The element also stops sticking once its parent scrolls out of view.
interviewTips: List the three requirements for sticky to work.

title: CSS Cascade Layers (@layer)
question: What problem do CSS cascade layers (@layer) solve?
answer: `@layer` creates explicit priority tiers in the cascade. Layers declared later win over earlier ones regardless of selector specificity, giving predictable control over third-party vs app styles.
explanation: You order layers once (e.g. reset, base, components, utilities), then specificity only matters within a layer. Unlayered styles rank above all layers.
bestPractices: Declare layer order once at the top.
Put utilities in a late layer.
interviewTips: Explain how layers change specificity resolution.

title: Grid Template Areas and gap
question: How do grid-template-areas and the gap property improve layout?
answer: `grid-template-areas` lets you name regions and place items visually with `grid-area`, producing readable, self-documenting layouts. `gap` sets consistent spacing between tracks without margins.
explanation: Named areas make responsive rearrangement trivial: redefine the areas string in a media query. gap replaces fragile margin hacks and collapses cleanly.
bestPractices: Use named areas for readable page layouts.
Prefer gap over margins for grid spacing.
commonMistakes: Using margins where gap is cleaner.
interviewTips: Show rearranging areas inside a media query.

title: Logical Properties
question: What are CSS logical properties and why use them?
answer: Logical properties (`margin-inline`, `padding-block`, `inset-inline-start`) map to the writing direction rather than physical sides, so layouts adapt automatically for RTL and vertical writing modes.
explanation: Instead of margin-left, use margin-inline-start; it flips correctly in right-to-left locales without extra overrides, improving internationalization.
bestPractices: Prefer logical properties for i18n-ready UIs.
interviewTips: Explain inline vs block axis mapping.

title: will-change and Rendering Performance
question: What does the will-change property do and when should you use it?
answer: `will-change` hints to the browser to promote an element to its own compositor layer ahead of an expected change (transform, opacity), reducing jank. Overusing it wastes memory and can hurt performance.
explanation: Apply it just before an animation and remove it after. Blanket usage forces many layers, exhausting GPU memory and negating the benefit.
bestPractices: Apply sparingly and remove after animating.
commonMistakes: Setting will-change on many elements permanently.
Using it as a fix for unrelated jank.
interviewTips: Discuss layer promotion and memory trade-offs.

title: Dark Mode with prefers-color-scheme
question: How do you implement dark mode with prefers-color-scheme and custom properties?
answer: Detect the user's OS preference with the `prefers-color-scheme` media query and override CSS custom properties for colors. This themes the whole UI by swapping token values.
explanation: Pairing custom properties with the media query gives a single source of truth. Add `color-scheme` so form controls and scrollbars adapt too, and optionally allow a manual toggle via a data attribute.
interviewTips: Mention combining OS preference with a manual override.
